agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Edmonds traffic and speed review
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to consider a proposed traffic and speed management review for residential streets in the Edmonds area. The review includes collecting data and analyzing traffic patterns, and staff will also draft new bylaws to help monitor and enforce speed limits. This is not yet approved — the decision is up for a future council vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend to voice your concerns about speeding in your neighbourhood.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.1
Anthon Bakkalian - Residential Speeding Crisis in Edmonds
1.
Anthon Bakkalian - Residential Speeding Crisis in Edmonds.pdf
2.
Attachment 1 - Letter to Mayor and Council.pdf
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Community Safety, General Manager Engineering
Note from General Manager Engineering
: Staff are currently conducting a traffic and speed management review, including data collection and analysis, along these corridors. Bylaws will also be informed to monitor and enforce vehicles travelling above the speed limit within these areas.

attachments.title: Anthon Bakkalian - Residential Speeding Crisis in Edmonds.pdf

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to extend temporary shelter at 7320 Buller Avenue through summer 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider extending the temporary shelter and emergency warming centre at 7320 Buller Avenue to operate year-round through summer 2026, after receiving new funding from BC Housing. The shelter was previously only open in winter. Neighbours have complained about little notice of the change, increased property crime, garbage, and tents near the shelter. A community meeting is set for May 20 to discuss concerns. The City is considering steps like a garbage pickup program, a crime prevention review, and more RCMP patrols.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the community meeting on May 20 to share your concerns about the shelter extension.

key_dates.label: Community meeting on Buller Avenue shelter extension — Burnaby

agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.2
Temporary Shelter and Emergency Warming Centre at 7320 Buller Avenue
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Engineering, General Manager Planning and Development, General Manager Community Safety
Note from General Manager Community Safety:
Issue:
The City has recently received several complaints from the community regarding the Buller Street shelter and the decision to extend the operations through the summer of 2026 as it has just received funding from BC Housing to do so. This is a change in operating procedures as this shelter was previously only open over the winter as it did not have funding from BC Housing to operate during the summer months. The complaints relate to little notice on the change to the operating schedule, increased property crime in the neighborhood, more garbage in the area as well as some unhoused community members living in tents in close proximity to the shelter and the fact that those who purchased property in the area were advised that the Shelter would be demolished and transformed into a park.
Current Status:
Staff from Community Safety, Planning and the RCMP have met with the service provider at the shelter in an effort to understand the issues that are occurring in and around the shelter as well as to share information from community members that have come forward in relation to the expanded hours of operation through the summer. A community meeting is scheduled for May 20 involving the Community Safety Department and members of the community. It is expected that the writer will be in attendance at this community meeting. The RCMP have been involved in order to assess how prevalent reports of crime and social unrest have been in this neighbourhood and to investigate/address any reports of crime whether that be property related crime or incidents of violence. The tents outside of the shelter were removed and cleaned up after significant efforts were made to provide shelter services to the individual involved at that location. The ISW Team along with the RCMP, Bylaws and Engineering were all involved in finding a solution for this reported issue. With that said, it appears that a new encampment may have appeared over this past weekend and the ISW has engaged once again.
Future Considerations:
Subject to the information that will be received at the May 20
th
meeting and confirmation of the issues, the City of Burnaby will be determining how to best support the shelter and the community around the shelter. The City has initiated several strategies in order to support the community that include:
Implement a garbage pick up program in the neighbourhood around the shelter - Engineering Solid Waste will lead this initiative upon confirmation of this issue.
Staff will provide neighbours with an information sheet that will help direct community members to know who to call if an issue arises.
A Crime Prevention through Environmental Design has been conducted around the shelter site to determine what could be done to mitigate threats of criminal behaviour and social unrest.
RCMP Community Response Team is monitoring the area and will look to determine if there has been an increase in reported crimes in the area.
The ISW team will continue to patrol the area and offer services to unsheltered community members who may be setting up in tents in this neighborhood.
The Crime Prevention Team is engaged in order to connect neighbours with resources to continue to support the community.
Engagement with Translink and the Province should it be determined that some of the complaints regarding tents in the neighborhood are on Provincial land.
Staff from Planning and Community Safety have met with BC Housing and Progressive and will remain in contact as this matter transitions and is resolved. While the news of the extended shelter operation through the summer means that up to 40 individuals will not become unsheltered this summer, it has created some concern in the community. All agency partners will be working to alleviate these concerns brought forth by community members.
agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 4-unit development at 6516 Portland Street under building permit review
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to consider a building permit application for a 4-unit housing development at 6516 Portland Street. The application was received in March 2026 and must follow the amended R1 Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing District rules. Because this is a 'non-discretional' permit — meaning the City cannot refuse it if the plans meet all zoning, building code, and other city bylaws — the City is required to approve it if it is compliant. The decision is not final yet; the permit is still under review.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you at this time — the permit is under review and will be approved if it meets all rules.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.3
Development at 6516 Portland Street
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Planning and Development
Note from General Manager Planning and Development:
A Building Permit Application has been received and is under review for a 4-unit development at 6516 Portland Street.  The application was received in March of 2026, and is required to be compliant with the amended R1 Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing District. As a non-discretional permit type, the City is unable to deny a building permit application that is compliant with the Zoning Bylaw and the BC Building Code, and meets all other bylaws of the City.
agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rosser Avenue and Halifax Street intersection changes in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a request from Penny Leclair to add a pedestrian signal for crossing Rosser Avenue at Halifax Street. The proposal is listed on the upcoming council meeting agenda and has not yet been approved. If passed, the change could affect traffic flow, parking, or pedestrian access in this area. Business owners near this intersection should watch for the council decision, as it may impact customer access or delivery routes.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on this intersection proposal — it could affect access to your business.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Lakers Junior A Lacrosse team booking rules at Copeland Arena
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal to formalize booking rules for the Burnaby Lakers Junior A Lacrosse team at Copeland Arena. Currently, the team has been reminded to stick to their booked times, but no fines exist for going over. Moving forward, extended use beyond booked times may be added to bookings, subject to operational, insurance, safety, and bylaw requirements. This is a proposed change — not yet approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you use Copeland Arena or similar city facilities, watch for the council decision — it could affect how extra time is billed or approved.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.2.1
Eugene Hibbert - Burnaby Lakers Junior A Lacrosse Team at Copeland Arena
1.
Eugene Hibbert -  Burnaby Lakers Junior A Lacrosse Team at Copeland Arena.pdf
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture
Note from Deputy General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture
: Staff will follow up with the writer regarding their concerns. The Junior A team has not received fines, as no fine process currently exists. They have been reminded to remain within their allocated booking times, consistent with all user groups. Moving forward, extended use beyond booked times may be added to bookings in accordance with operational, insurance, safety, and bylaw requirements.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: West Sells Park signage and speed bumps request
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a resident's request to install signage and speed bumps in the alley through West Sells Park (corner of MacDonald Avenue and Frances Street) to improve safety for children. The alley is used by drivers speeding through the park, which has a toddler play structure on one side and a grassy area on the other. No decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decision — if approved, new traffic calming measures could affect nearby streets and park access.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: New crosswalk across North Road at Still Creek Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a request from resident Connie Elder to install a new crosswalk across North Road at Still Creek Avenue, ideally with a signal light. The proposal also requests a crosswalk at Clarke Road, noting no crosswalks exist between Broadway and Casewell Street. The decision has not been made yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to consider federal Build Canada Homes letter
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is reviewing a letter from the federal Minister confirming that Build Canada Homes has received the Burnaby Housing Authority's submission for a proposed 1,200-home mixed-income rental project. The federal agency will review the proposal, may request missing documentation, and will assign a Partnerships Manager once the submission is deemed complete. No decision has been made by Council yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council meeting date — this could affect future housing development opportunities in Burnaby.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: BC Provincial Sales Tax expansion — Council to send letter to Minister
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council will consider sending a letter to the BC Minister of Finance, drafted by Abbotsford, opposing the proposed expansion of PST to professional and related services. The letter requests either an exemption for local governments or a rebate/offset mechanism similar to the GST model, citing significant cumulative financial impacts on municipal capital and operating budgets.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council vote — if the letter is sent, it could signal future PST changes affecting businesses that provide professional services to municipalities.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to consider SSMUH rules and restrictive covenants
agenda_briefs.summary: On April 28, 2026, Burnaby Council voted 5–4 against a resolution that would have exempted properties with restrictive covenants from Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing (SSMUH) rules. A restrictive covenant is a legal promise in a property deed that can limit what you build — for example, banning anything other than a single-family home. The vote means SSMUH rules allowing duplexes, triplexes, and other multi-unit housing in single-family zones will apply even where a private covenant restricts development. Council had previously directed staff on September 9, 2025 to exempt the Brentwood Park neighbourhood until covenants were removed, but the April 28 staff report recommended against that approach and Council agreed.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check whether your property has a restrictive covenant — if it does, you may need to legally remove it before building under SSMUH rules.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote on SSMUH restrictive-covenant exemption

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby-North Shore Rapid Bus Transit project correspondence
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider correspondence from Carolyn Mitchell opposing the Hastings Street BRT option and supporting Option 2 along Boundary Road. The correspondence notes Hastings Street expansion to six lanes would harm local businesses and walkability, while Boundary Road already has a wide median and less residential impact. No decision has been made; the project remains unapproved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor council decisions on the BRT route — a Hastings Street option could affect customer access and parking for Burnaby Heights businesses.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to consider support for chlorine gas concerns
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposal to support concerns about Chemtrade Electrochem Inc.'s shipping of liquid chlorine gas through Burnaby. An average of 4 pressurized rail cars carrying liquid chlorine gas travel through Burnaby daily, with 32 rail cars parked at the North Vancouver plant. A risk assessment estimates a worst-case chlorine gas release could cause 2,000 fatalities, 1,230 severe injuries, and 12,300 hospitalizations.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect businesses that use or transport chlorine gas in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider correspondence from Descendants of Komagata Maru Society
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is considering correspondence from the Descendants of the Komagata Maru Society, which demands that the Society's name be added to the new draft Komagata Maru interpretive sign. The Society states it will not accept the sign, will not attend the unveiling event, and will oppose the sign unless the name is restored. The Society's vice president notes the old sign draft included the name and that other cities (Port Moody, North Delta, Richmond, Mission, New Westminster, Surrey, Abbotsford) have included the Society's name on their signs.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Rathburn Drive student housing rezoning
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on REZ #25-19 to rezone 9971 Rathburn Drive for a student accommodation building. The proposal was previously voted down on October 28, 2025. The mayor cited concerns about the ecologically sensitive area, salmon creek impacts, lack of solid SFU connection, and the project being too large for the neighbourhood. The developer (Forum) may submit a new application six months after the vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit a written comment to Burnaby Council before the vote to share your support or concerns.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Rathburn Drive housing rezoning (REZ #25-19)
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a housing proposal for Rathburn Drive (REZ #25-19) that would rezone the property for single room occupancy (SRO) housing. The proposal was previously rejected by Council. Residents Jenny and Norman Lum have submitted a letter opposing the plan, citing concerns about a single entry/exit point for emergency evacuation, limited parking, potential pollutants washing into salmon-bearing Stoney Creek, and lack of disclosed rental rates compared to nearby student housing. The developer has imposed non-disclosure agreements on 16 households benefiting from the venture. A final decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments to have your say on this proposed rezoning.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to decide on building permit dispute for 5970 Sussex Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider a building permit dispute involving James Jiaming Wu for the property at 5970 Sussex Avenue. Neighbors object to a proposed 6-unit townhouse development on a narrow lot, citing a 4-story building (including basement) set only 3 feet from the neighbor's fence, 4 parking spaces for 6 units, and insufficient street frontage (approx. 33 feet) for required garbage bins. The agenda item includes a report from the Chief Administrative Officer and the General Managers of Corporate Services and Planning and Development. This is a proposed decision — Council has not yet voted on it, and the outcome could affect the property owner's ability to proceed with construction or renovations under the existing permit.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you are the property owner or have an interest in 5970 Sussex Avenue, attend the Council meeting or submit written comments before the vote.

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to consider new rules for street naming and correspondence
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal called 'Burnaby Needs More Streets' that could change how the city handles street naming and related correspondence. The proposal is set to be discussed at an upcoming council meeting. If approved, it may affect how new streets are named and how the city communicates with residents and businesses about street-related matters. The proposal is still under review and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider submitting your feedback on the proposed street naming changes.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to consider Michael J. Fox Theatre closure
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to close the Michael J. Fox Theatre. The decision is based on correspondence and a report from the Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager of Corporate Services, and the Deputy General Manager of Parks, Recreation and Culture. If approved, this would mean the theatre would no longer operate as a public venue. Business owners who rely on the theatre for events, performances, or community gatherings should be aware that this change could affect their operations or rental options.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Contact your Burnaby Council representative to express your views before the vote.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.11
Michael J. Fox Theatre Closure
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, Deputy General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture
agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Metrotown pedestrian scramble
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a request from Nikki Best to install a pedestrian scramble at a Metrotown intersection. A pedestrian scramble is a crosswalk pattern where all vehicle traffic stops at once, allowing people to walk diagonally across the intersection. This proposal is on the agenda for an upcoming meeting and has not yet been approved. If passed, it would change how pedestrians and drivers move through this busy area near Metrotown.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect traffic and foot traffic near your business.

agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on paving Burnaby Lake trails
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider a proposal to pave the trails around Burnaby Lake. This has sparked opposition from residents and community groups who want to keep the trails natural. A proposed bylaw or staff recommendation on this matter is up for discussion at an upcoming council meeting. If approved, the paving could change how the trails are used for walking, cycling, and recreation. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Contact your Burnaby councillor before the vote to express your support or opposition to the paving plan.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.13
Opposition to Paving the Burnaby Lake Trails
Copy: Chief Administrative Officer, General Manager Corporate Services, General Manager Engineering, General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture
agenda_briefs.article_title: Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions Agenda — May 26, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: NAV Canada flight path changes — Burnaby Council to consider response
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider a proposed response to NAV Canada's flight path changes, which were implemented between December 2025 and February 2026. A resident's letter reports that noise levels in North Burnaby have reached over 65 dB(A), exceeding NAV Canada's predicted maximum of 55 dB(A) — a perceived doubling of loudness. The letter requests an independent post-implementation review including noise monitoring, flight path revisions, and possible overnight curfews or compensation for affected neighbourhoods.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Contact your Burnaby City Councillor before the vote to share your views on the proposed flight path changes.
agenda_briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to consider 'Density Without Demolition' pilot program
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal from the View Royal Climate Coalition to launch a 'Density Without Demolition' (DWD) pilot program. This proposed roadmap would allow homeowners to add secondary suites or garden suites without tearing down existing homes. The goal is to create more housing while keeping older, affordable homes standing. The proposal is on the agenda for discussion — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect how you add rental units to your property.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Temporary Use Permit for church at 7375 Kingsway
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council will vote on Temporary Use Permit #26-06 for Southside Community Church at 7375 Kingsway. The permit would allow social service centre, religious assembly, and public assembly uses for three years on property owned by Beem Credit Union. Written comments must be received by noon on May 25, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written comments referencing TUP #26-06 by email to legislativeservices@burnaby.ca, via webform at Burnaby.ca/SubmitALetter, or by mail/hand delivery to Mayor and Council c/o Legislative Services by noon on May 25, 2026.
